sections.heading: Why Code Quality and Best Practices Matter
sections.content: Code quality is not just about writing code that works — it is about writing code that is maintainable, testable, readable, and resilient over time. Android apps are long-lived products that grow in complexity, change hands between developers, and must adapt to new Android versions, new devices, and evolving user expectations. Poor code quality leads to bugs that are hard to reproduce, features that are expensive to add, regressions that slip into production, and developer burnout from working in a codebase that fights you at every turn. Adopting consistent best practices — clean architecture, meaningful naming, small focused classes, comprehensive testing, and rigorous code review — compounds over time into a codebase that remains productive and enjoyable to work in years after its creation. Best practices are not rules for their own sake; they are accumulated wisdom from countless teams who learned what works and what causes pain at scale.

rows.description: Keep UI logic in ViewModels, business logic in UseCases, and data access in Repositories — each layer has a single responsibility
rows.anti_pattern: Putting network calls, database queries, or business logic directly in Activities or Fragments

rows.description: UI observes state from ViewModel; user actions flow up to ViewModel; ViewModel updates state; state flows down to UI — never the reverse
rows.anti_pattern: UI directly modifying shared state or calling repository methods; ViewModel holding references to the UI

rows.description: Each piece of data has exactly one authoritative source — the Repository is the single source of truth for all data in the app
rows.anti_pattern: Maintaining the same data in multiple places (ViewModel and Fragment both holding separate copies of the user list)

rows.description: Inject dependencies through constructors rather than creating them inside classes — makes code testable and modular
rows.anti_pattern: Instantiating Retrofit, Room, or Repository objects with 'new' inside Activity or ViewModel

rows.description: Define Repository and DataSource as interfaces; inject concrete implementations — allows fake implementations in tests
rows.anti_pattern: Depending directly on concrete classes (UserRepositoryImpl) instead of their interface (UserRepository)

sections.heading: Kotlin Code Quality
items: Prefer val over var — immutability prevents accidental state mutation and makes code easier to reason about
Use data classes for models — automatic equals(), hashCode(), copy(), and toString() with no boilerplate
Use sealed classes for exhaustive state representation: sealed class UiState { object Loading; data class Success(val data: List<Item>); data class Error(val message: String) }
Prefer extension functions over utility classes — keeps logic close to the type it operates on and reads naturally
Use scope functions appropriately: let for null-safe operations, apply for object configuration, run for object transformation, also for side effects, with for grouping calls
Avoid !! (non-null assertion operator) — it is a deferred NullPointerException; use safe calls (?.), Elvis operator (?:), or require()/checkNotNull() with a meaningful message
Use named arguments for functions with multiple parameters of the same type: sendMessage(to = recipient, from = sender) — eliminates parameter order bugs
Prefer when expressions over if-else chains for multiple conditions — more readable and exhaustive for sealed classes and enums

rows.examples: companion object { const val MAX_RETRY_COUNT = 3; const val BASE_URL = "https://api.example.com" }

rows.convention: Underscore prefix for private mutable; public val without underscore
component_type_description.xml — snake_case with component type prefix
componentType_description — camelCase in code; snake_case in XML
rows.examples: btnSubmit / btn_submit, tvUserName / tv_user_name, rvProductList / rv_product_list

rows.convention: Backtick descriptive names: given_when_then or method_scenario_expectedResult
rows.examples: `given valid input, when submit clicked, then navigates to home`

sections.heading: ViewModel Best Practices
items: Expose immutable state to the UI: use StateFlow<UiState> via asStateFlow() — never expose MutableStateFlow directly
Use a single UiState sealed class per screen — reduces the complexity of managing multiple LiveData/Flow properties
Handle one-shot events (navigation, toasts, dialogs) via SharedFlow or Channel — never via StateFlow which replays the last value
Never hold a reference to Context, View, Activity, or Fragment in a ViewModel — it causes memory leaks and couples ViewModel to the UI lifecycle
Use viewModelScope for all coroutines — it is automatically cancelled when the ViewModel is cleared
Catch exceptions at the ViewModel layer and map them to user-friendly error states — never let raw exceptions propagate to the UI
Keep ViewModels focused on a single screen — if a ViewModel is used by multiple screens it is probably doing too much
Use SavedStateHandle for arguments passed via navigation — it survives process death and is testable without navigation infrastructure

rows.description: Always launch coroutines within a scope that has a defined lifecycle — viewModelScope, lifecycleScope, or a custom scope
rows.anti_pattern: Using GlobalScope — it has no lifecycle and coroutines leak until the process dies

rows.description: Dispatchers.Main for UI, Dispatchers.IO for disk and network, Dispatchers.Default for CPU-intensive work
rows.anti_pattern: Running network or database operations on Dispatchers.Main — causes ANR and NetworkOnMainThreadException

rows.description: Switch dispatcher for a block of work with withContext(Dispatchers.IO) { } rather than launching a new coroutine
rows.anti_pattern: launch(Dispatchers.IO) { } inside a suspend function — creates an unstructured child coroutine

rows.description: Wrap callback-based APIs with suspendCancellableCoroutine { } — integrates cleanly with coroutine cancellation
rows.anti_pattern: Mixing callbacks and coroutines — creates unpredictable execution order and difficult error handling

rows.description: Check isActive in long loops; use ensureActive(); propagate CancellationException — never catch and swallow it
rows.anti_pattern: catch (e: Exception) { } that also catches CancellationException — prevents coroutine cancellation from propagating

rows.practice: Use Flow for streams, suspend for single values
rows.description: Return Flow<T> for data that changes over time; return T or Result<T> from suspend functions for one-shot operations
rows.anti_pattern: Returning LiveData from Repository — LiveData is a UI concern; Repository should return Flow

sections.heading: UI and Compose Best Practices
items: Keep composables stateless where possible — lift state up to the ViewModel or a stateful parent composable
Use state hoisting: pass state down as parameters and events up as lambda callbacks — makes composables reusable and testable
Avoid reading from ViewModel or doing business logic inside composables — pass pre-computed state via UiState and event callbacks
Use remember { } for expensive calculations that should survive recomposition but not configuration changes
Use rememberSaveable { } for state that should survive both recomposition and configuration changes
Mark lambdas as stable with remember { } to prevent unnecessary recompositions when parent recomposes
Use LazyColumn/LazyRow for lists — never Column with forEach for dynamic lists of arbitrary size

Apply Modifier parameters as the first parameter after content for composable functions: fun MyComposable(modifier: Modifier = Modifier, content: @Composable () -> Unit) — allows callers to customize layout

rows.description: Wrap repository return values in Result<T> or a custom sealed class — makes error handling explicit and type-safe at every call site
rows.example: sealed class DataResult<T> { data class Success<T>(val data: T); data class Error<T>(val exception: Exception) }

rows.description: Always handle or rethrow exceptions — catch blocks that log and return null hide bugs and make debugging nearly impossible
rows.example: catch (e: IOException) { _uiState.value = UiState.Error(e.message ?: "Unknown error") } — map to UI state

rows.description: Convert low-level exceptions (IOException, HttpException) to meaningful domain error types in the Repository — ViewModel should not know about HTTP status codes
rows.example: catch (e: HttpException) { if (e.code() == 401) throw UnauthorizedException() else throw NetworkException() }

rows.description: Attach a CoroutineExceptionHandler to catch unhandled exceptions from launch { } blocks as a last resort safety net
rows.example: val handler = CoroutineExceptionHandler { _, e -> _uiState.value = UiState.Error(e.message) }; viewModelScope.launch(handler) { }

rows.description: Implement retry logic with increasing delays for transient network failures — do not hammer a failing server
rows.example: repeat(MAX_RETRIES) { attempt -> try { return api.fetch() } catch (e: IOException) { delay(2.0.pow(attempt).toLong() * 1000) } }

sections.heading: Dependency Management Best Practices
items: Use Gradle version catalogs (libs.versions.toml) to centralize all dependency versions — eliminates version conflicts and scattered version declarations
Define versions in [versions], libraries in [libraries], and plugins in [plugins] sections of libs.versions.toml
Reference in build.gradle.kts: implementation(libs.retrofit) — type-safe, IDE-auto-completed dependency declarations
Use BOM (Bill of Materials) for library families: implementation(platform(libs.firebase.bom)) — ensures all Firebase libraries use compatible versions
Regularly update dependencies — outdated libraries accumulate security vulnerabilities and compatibility issues
Use Renovate or Dependabot to automate dependency update PRs — reduces manual tracking overhead
Audit transitive dependencies with: ./gradlew app:dependencies — understand what your libraries bring in
Prefer official Jetpack libraries over third-party alternatives when equivalent functionality exists — better lifecycle integration and long-term support

rows.description: Each pull request should implement one feature or fix one bug — large PRs are hard to review thoroughly and hide bugs
rows.guideline: Aim for under 400 lines changed per PR; split larger features into sequential smaller PRs

rows.description: Explain what changed, why it changed, how to test it, and any risks — reviewers should understand context without reading every line
rows.guideline: Include: summary of changes, screenshots for UI changes, test instructions, and link to the issue or ticket

rows.description: Automated tools (ktlint, detekt) handle style — reviewers should focus on logic, architecture, edge cases, and security
rows.guideline: Configure ktlint and detekt in CI to block PRs with style violations automatically

rows.description: Comment on the code, not the author; explain why a change is needed, not just that it is wrong; suggest alternatives
rows.guideline: Use conventional comments: 'nit:' for minor style, 'question:' for clarification, 'blocker:' for must-fix issues

rows.description: Every new ViewModel method, Repository function, or UseCase should have corresponding unit tests before merging
rows.guideline: Make test coverage a PR requirement — configure minimum coverage thresholds in CI

rows.practice: Use feature flags for unfinished work
rows.description: Merge incomplete features behind a feature flag rather than using long-lived feature branches — keeps the main branch releasable at all times
rows.guideline: Use BuildConfig.FEATURE_ENABLED or remote feature flags (Firebase Remote Config) to control feature visibility

sections.heading: Static Analysis Tools
items: ktlint — enforces Kotlin coding style and formatting; run locally: ./gradlew ktlintCheck; fix automatically: ./gradlew ktlintFormat
Add ktlint to build.gradle.kts: plugins { id("org.jlleitschuh.gradle.ktlint") version "12.x.x" }
detekt — static analysis for Kotlin code quality: complexity, style, potential bugs, performance issues
Add detekt: plugins { id("io.gitlab.arturbosch.detekt") version "1.x.x" }; run: ./gradlew detekt
Configure detekt rules in detekt.yml — customize thresholds for complexity metrics like cyclomatic complexity and method length
Android Lint — built-in Android Studio tool detecting: missing translations, accessibility issues, deprecated API usage, performance problems
Run Lint: ./gradlew lint — generates an HTML report at build/reports/lint-results-debug.html
Configure Lint in build.gradle.kts: android { lint { abortOnError = true; warningsAsErrors = true; htmlReport = true } }
Add all static analysis checks to CI — PRs that fail ktlint, detekt, or Lint checks should not be merged

rows.description: Adopt GitFlow or trunk-based development — main/master is always releasable; feature branches are short-lived
Follow Conventional Commits: type(scope): description — enables automated changelog generation
rows.implementation: feat(login): add biometric authentication; fix(cart): correct total calculation for discounted items; chore(deps): update Retrofit to 2.11.0

rows.description: Run build, unit tests, static analysis, and lint on every pull request — prevent broken code from reaching main
rows.implementation: GitHub Actions, Bitrise, or CircleCI workflow: build > test > lint > detekt > ktlint

rows.description: Build, sign, and upload release AABs on CI using secure environment variables for signing credentials — no manual release builds
rows.implementation: Trigger on merge to main or version tags; use fastlane supply or Gradle Play Publisher to upload to Play Console

rows.description: Never commit: local.properties, *.jks keystore files, google-services.json, .idea/, build/ directories, API keys
rows.implementation: Use the standard Android .gitignore template from gitignore.io or GitHub's Android template

sections.heading: Memory and Resource Management
items: Always clear View references in Fragment.onDestroyView(): private var _binding: FragmentHomeBinding? = null — prevents the most common Fragment memory leak
Use lifecycleScope and viewModelScope instead of GlobalScope — automatically cancelled when the lifecycle ends
Unregister listeners, observers, and BroadcastReceivers in the corresponding lifecycle cleanup method — onStop(), onDestroyView(), or onDestroy()
Use LeakCanary in debug builds to detect memory leaks automatically: debugImplementation("com.squareup.leakcanary:leakcanary-android:2.x")
Avoid static references to Context, Activity, View, or any object that holds them — use applicationContext for singletons
Use Glide or Coil for image loading — they handle caching, memory management, and lifecycle awareness automatically
Cancel image loading requests when a View is recycled or a Fragment is destroyed — Coil and Glide handle this automatically when used with the correct lifecycle owner
Close resources explicitly: database connections, streams, and Cursor objects must always be closed in finally blocks or use use { }

rows.description: 70% unit tests, 20% integration tests, 10% UI tests — fast, reliable unit tests are the foundation
rows.tool: JUnit + Mockito + Turbine for unit; Room in-memory + Hilt for integration; Espresso + Compose Testing for UI

rows.description: Assert what the code does from the outside — avoid testing private methods or verifying every internal call
rows.tool: Write tests from the perspective of the caller; refactor freely without breaking tests

rows.description: Tests must produce the same result every time — eliminate randomness, time dependencies, and shared mutable state
rows.tool: Use fake clocks, fixed test data, and isolated in-memory databases

rows.description: Test loading, success, error, empty, and edge case states — not just the happy path
rows.tool: Turbine for asserting state sequences; fake repositories with controllable error injection

rows.description: Unit tests run on every PR; instrumented tests run nightly or on release branches
rows.tool: GitHub Actions for unit tests; Firebase Test Lab or Gradle Managed Devices for instrumented tests

sections.heading: Documentation and Code Readability
items: Write self-documenting code — choose names that make the intent obvious; prefer readable code over comments that explain unclear code
Add KDoc comments to all public APIs: /** * Returns the user with the given ID, or null if not found. * @param userId The unique identifier of the user. */
Document the 'why', not the 'what' — comments should explain non-obvious decisions, constraints, or workarounds, not restate what the code does
Keep functions short and focused — if a function does not fit on one screen it is doing too much; extract helper functions
Avoid magic numbers and magic strings — extract them as named constants: const val MAX_RETRY_COUNT = 3 instead of repeat(3) { }
Use TODO() with context: TODO("Replace with real API call after backend is ready — tracked in JIRA-123") — actionable and traceable
Delete dead code immediately — commented-out code adds noise and creates confusion about whether it should exist; version control preserves history
Keep files focused — if a file exceeds ~300 lines it likely needs to be split into smaller, more focused classes

rows.description: A single Activity or Fragment that handles network calls, business logic, database access, and UI — hundreds or thousands of lines long
rows.fix: Extract ViewModels for state management, Repositories for data, and UseCases for business logic — Activity and Fragment should only handle UI

rows.description: Passing Activity context to singletons, static fields, or long-lived objects — the Activity cannot be garbage collected
rows.fix: Use applicationContext for singletons; never store Activity reference in anything that outlives it

rows.description: Deeply nested callbacks for async operations — hard to read, error-prone, and difficult to cancel or handle errors in
rows.fix: Replace callbacks with coroutines and suspend functions — flat, linear code with structured error handling

rows.description: Optimizing code before measuring — wasting time on micro-optimizations that have no meaningful impact on user experience
rows.fix: Profile first with Android Studio Profiler; optimize only bottlenecks that are measured to impact performance

rows.description: Storing UI state in Activity fields that are lost on rotation — app resets state and restarts network requests on every configuration change
rows.fix: Store all UI state in ViewModel which survives configuration changes; use SavedStateHandle for process death

rows.description: Using android:text="Submit" directly in XML — untranslatable, inconsistent, and violates the single source of truth principle
rows.fix: Always use string resources: android:text="@string/common_submit" — enables localization and consistency

rows.description: Suppressing all R8 warnings with -dontwarn ** — hides real issues that cause runtime crashes in release builds
rows.fix: Resolve each warning individually; add specific -dontwarn rules only for intentionally absent optional dependencies

sections.heading: Staying Current with Android Development
items: Follow the official Android Developers blog: android-developers.googleblog.com — announcements, best practice guides, and deep dives from the Android team
Watch Android at Google I/O sessions annually — major platform updates, new Jetpack APIs, and Compose advancements are announced here
Read the official Android Architecture Guides: developer.android.com/topic/architecture — the canonical guidance from Google on MVVM, UDF, and Jetpack usage
Follow Now in Android — Google's official weekly digest of Android developer news, blog posts, and videos
Use the Android Studio preview channel to stay ahead of IDE improvements and new inspections: Help > Check for Updates > switch to Canary channel
Keep dependencies updated — Jetpack libraries receive regular improvements; outdated versions miss bug fixes and Compose optimizations
Contribute to or read open source Android projects — AOSP, Now in Android sample app, and other Google sample apps demonstrate current best practices
Join the Kotlin and Android developer communities: KotlinLang Slack, Android Dev subreddit, Droidcon conference talks — real-world experience shared by practitioners

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: Android best practices and code quality are the cumulative result of consistent discipline applied across architecture, Kotlin usage, testing, tooling, and team processes. Build on a solid MVVM foundation with unidirectional data flow, single sources of truth, and dependency injection. Write idiomatic Kotlin — prefer val, use sealed classes for exhaustive state, avoid !!, and leverage scope functions appropriately. Keep ViewModels free of Android framework references, expose immutable state via StateFlow, and handle all errors explicitly with meaningful UI state. Test thoroughly at all three levels of the pyramid, with the majority of coverage in fast unit tests using JUnit, Mockito, and Turbine. Enforce code quality automatically with ktlint, detekt, and Android Lint integrated into CI so that style and quality gates are consistent and unambiguous. Manage dependencies centrally with version catalogs, write meaningful commit messages and PR descriptions, and keep the main branch always releasable. The best Android codebases are not built in a single sprint — they are the result of small, consistent, disciplined decisions made every day.
